coredata.dc:title: Encapsulating site-directly carbonized CDs in MOF(Cr) as adsorption-photothermal sites for boosting toluene Ad/de-sorption process via photo-assisted strategy

coredata.dc:description: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) require high energy for the desorption of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) due to their strong adsorption affinity. For this purpose, a photo-assisted desorption is proposed for MOF regeneration. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) with efficient light absorption and thermal conductivity are encapsulated in MOFs using the “site-directly carbonization” approach, forming ship-in-a-bottle o-CDs-M101. These CDs can act as “VOCs affinity sites” and “photothermal spots” in MOFs, simultaneously maintaining toluene adsorption capacity, while enhancing desorption efficiency under humid conditions. Consequently, the designed o-CDs-M101 (2558 m2/g) achieves a 40 % increase in toluene uptake compared to the original MIL-101(Cr), reaching up to 3.6 mmol/g under 400 ppm and RH = 60 %. During the desorption process, CDs rapidly generate heat/electrons under light radiation to attack the adsorbed VOCs and make them quickly and thoroughly desorb. Owing to this attribute, it shows 3.4 and 3.8 times faster desorption rate and higher concentration ratio under photo-assisted conditions at 110 °C compared to thermal conditions. Interestingly, these adsorbed H2O further dramatically accelerate toluene desorption from MOFs, up to 2.5 times faster compared to water-free conditions. This approach is also broadly applicable to HKUST-1 and MIL-100(Fe), achieving similar high efficiency in toluene photo-assisted desorption. The findings demonstrate that the introduction of photothermal sites offers a promising approach to MOF regeneration with high efficiency and low energy consumption.

SEM images of (a) MIL-101(Cr) and (b) o-CDs-M101; TEM image of (c) o-CDs-M101 and (d) CDs from o-CDs-M101; SEM images of (e) p-CDs-M101 and (f) CA-M101; (g) PXRD pattern, (h) FTIR spectra, (i) Raman spectra, (j) TGA and DTG, (k) N2 isotherms/pore size distribution (Horvath-Kawazoe model) and (l) peak area ratio of C-C (C sp2 and C sp3)/C-O of MIL-101(Cr), CA-M101, p-CDs-M101 and o-CDs-M101.

head.ce:title: Encapsulating site-directly carbonized CDs in MOF(Cr) as adsorption-photothermal sites for boosting toluene Ad/de-sorption process via photo-assisted strategy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) require high energy for the desorption of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) due to their strong adsorption affinity. For this purpose, a photo-assisted desorption is proposed for MOF regeneration. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) with efficient light absorption and thermal conductivity are encapsulated in MOFs using the “site-directly carbonization” approach, forming ship-in-a-bottle o-CDs-M101. These CDs can act as “VOCs affinity sites” and “photothermal spots” in MOFs, simultaneously maintaining toluene adsorption capacity, while enhancing desorption efficiency under humid conditions. Consequently, the designed o-CDs-M101 (2558 m2/g) achieves a 40 % increase in toluene uptake compared to the original MIL-101(Cr), reaching up to 3.6 mmol/g under 400 ppm and RH = 60 %. During the desorption process, CDs rapidly generate heat/electrons under light radiation to attack the adsorbed VOCs and make them quickly and thoroughly desorb. Owing to this attribute, it shows 3.4 and 3.8 times faster desorption rate and higher concentration ratio under photo-assisted conditions at 110 °C compared to thermal conditions. Interestingly, these adsorbed H2O further dramatically accelerate toluene desorption from MOFs, up to 2.5 times faster compared to water-free conditions. This approach is also broadly applicable to HKUST-1 and MIL-100(Fe), achieving similar high efficiency in toluene photo-assisted desorption. The findings demonstrate that the introduction of photothermal sites offers a promising approach to MOF regeneration with high efficiency and low energy consumption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Volatile organic compounds (VOCs), as one of the top three pollution sources, pose significant threats to both human health and surrounding environment [1,2]. These air contaminants not only trigger inflammation due to their odorous, carcinogenic, teratogenic and mutagenic natures [3,4], but also are photochemically reactive and fundamentally contribute to photochemical smog [5]. In various VOCs pollution cases, the emissions of benzene series VOCs from gasoline storage and distribution facilities have been identified as a major source of environmental concern, which leads to ecosystem damage and workplace safety hazards.[6,7]. Given the serious consequences of VOCs exposure, it is crucial to develop highly efficient methods for treating these VOCs emissions [8]. Among them, adsorption has emerged as a promising approach for VOC removal due to its low energy consumption, environmental friendliness and eco [9,10]. As is widely recognized, a critical element in successful adsorption processes is the use of adsorbents with exceptional selectivity for VOCs and high efficiency in the adsorption and regeneration processes [11].

In recent years, micro/mesoporous MOFs have gained attention for their feasibility in adsorbing VOCs attributed to their high specific surface area porosity, and density of active sites [12]. Previous efforts in surface and structural modification of MOFs have been proven to enhance MOF’s adsorption affinity, which could further enhance their VOCs capture abilities [13]. Thus, polymers or small molecules-such as polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) [11], polyethylene glycol (PEG) [14], polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) [15], benzoic acid [16] and 3,5-diaminobenzoic acid [17] etc. were implanted into the bulk of MOFs, resulting in increase in toluene adsorption capacity at low-concentrated compared to the pristine MOFs [17–19]. But in practical applications, the surface and structural modifications of MOFs with strong adsorption interaction has caused higher energy consumption for their regeneration and thus became a subsequent crucial limitation in their practical applications [20–22]. Currently, photo-assisted desorption has emerged as a novel strategy for regenerating VOC adsorbents based on its high efficiency of photothermal and photoelectric effects [23–25]. It exhibits higher energy transfer and conversion efficiency compared to pure thermal desorption [26]. Liu et al. used phenylenediamine as a photo-responsive ligand to implant into Zr-MOF to enhance its light-triggered heating efficiency for release of ethane. In order to enhance the light and heat conversion effects, Liu et al. incorporated Ag/GO nanoparticles into UiO-66/MIL-101 to enhance its light desorption efficiency for styrene and ethyl acetate [27,28]. However, whether implanted photo-responsive ligands, nanoparticle or nanosheets, severe decrease of adsorption sites are serious problems for these adsorbents. The trade-off effect between ad/desorption has become a vital issue. To tackle these issues, if an ultra-micro photothermal spot in MOFs adsorbents could enhance transfer energy to the absorbed molecules during light radiation without loss in toluene uptakes, a win–win situation for both adsorption capacity and desorption efficiency can be achieved.

In this work, a pioneering strategy is introduced, wherein carbon dots (CDs) endowed with photoelectronic traits are encapsulated within the cages of MIL-101(Cr) via the “site-directly carbonization” methodology. This innovative approach serves a dual purpose: it gives rise to “VOCs affinity sites” and “photothermic spots” that synergistically optimize the adsorption capacity for VOCs and expedite the desorption process, even under the challenging conditions of high humidity. During the designed one-pot “site-directly carbonization” synthesis protocol, CD precursors are initially coordinated with the metal ions of MOFs. Subsequently, they undergo site-directed carbonization concurrent with the growth of MIL-101(Cr). This process culminates in the formation of encapsulated CDs within the MIL-101(Cr) framework, employing the sophisticated ship-from-a-bottle strategy. The encapsulated CDs function as non-polar adsorption loci, selectively attracting toluene while repelling H2O. This unique property not only safeguards but also efficiently sustains a high adsorption uptake of toluene, a representative benzene series VOC, within humid environments. In the realm of photo-assisted desorption, the CDs within MOFs transform into highly efficient “photothermic spots.” They adeptly capture light energy and convert it into heat and electrons, thereby significantly augmenting the desorption of toluene from the MOFs’ pores. Notably, the involvement of water is experimentally verified to further catalyze the photothermal desorption of toluene from MOFs, providing an additional boost to the desorption efficiency. Moreover, the versatility of this CD site-directed carbonization approach is demonstrated by its seamless application to two quintessential MOFs, HKUST-1 and MIL-100(Fe). It consistently achieves high-efficiency photo-assisted desorption of toluene across these diverse MOF platforms. Additionally, a comprehensive exploration and proposition of a potential adsorption and desorption mechanism for the photo-swing sorption enhancement of VOCs over o-CDs-M101 is presented. This work provides a promising avenue for boosting desorption process towards microporous adsorbents having high VOCs affinity.

M101 crystals were prepared according to previously reported methods [14,29]. Briefly, equimolar amounts of Cr(NO3)3·9H2O (1 mmol) and H2BDC (1 mmol) were first dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water under stirring condition. After that, 0.9 μL HF (11 M) was added to the above solution and transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL) for the crystallization at 220 °C for 8 h. The green solid MIL-101(Cr) was collected by centrifugation and washed several times with DFM and ethanol to remove the surfactant and remained reactants.

o-CDs-M101 was one-pot synthesized by using similar procedure to M101 synthesis except for the 10 % extra addition of citric acid ((Cr(NO3)3·9H2O: CA mole ratio 10:1) in M101 precursor. Citric acid is the precursor of CDs and was in-situ synthesized in o-CDs-M101. The obtained samples were designated as o-CDs-M101.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contrast MOF composites synthesis of CA-M101 and p-CDs-M101
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the impact of CDs on the adsorption performance of MOFs and validate the universality of the synthesis method, the following comparative MOF composites were synthesized. To clarify the structure–function relationship of encapsulated CDs in M101 regarding VOCs sorption, the CDs monomer CA was pre-coordinated with Cr3+ at 120 °C for 3 h to prevent its polymerization into CD precursors within M101.The other procedures were similar with that of M101 synthesis. To investigate effects of CDs existence status on VOCs sorption properties, post-implantation of CDs in M101 (p-CDs-M101) was prepared by using dual-solvent strategy [30,31]. Shortly, a quantity of synthetic M101 was dispersed by sonication in 20 mL of hexane (the first solvent) for 30 min. Then, the precursor of CDs (2.1 g urea and 3.624 g anhydrous citric acid 6:1 mol rate) was dissolved into 10 mL deionized water. Subsequently, 0.2 mL solution B was dripped into MOF dispersion (100 mg in 20 mL deionized water) with vigorous stirring at 400 r/min for 12 h. Then the mixture was allowed to stand and pour out the supernatant. After the n-hexane evaporates entirely, the MOF solid powder was transferred into the tube furnace and calcine 8 h at N2 atmosphere. After cooling, the appropriate nanohybrids were denoted as p-CDs-M101.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of M100, o-CDs-M100, HKUST-1 and o-CDs-HK
ce:section.ce:para: MIL-100(Fe) (M100) and HKUST-1 (HK) were prepared according to previous method. M100 crystals were prepared with FeCl3·6H2O (2.5 mmol) and H2BDC (1.7 mmol) were firstly dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water under stirring condition. After that, 0.9 μL HF (11 M) and 0.68 μL HNO3 was added to the above solution and transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL) for the crystallization at 150 °C for 24 h. The orange solid was collected by centrifugation and washed several times with H2O and ethanol to remove the surfactant and remained reactants. This sample was named as M100. HKUST-1 crystals prepared with CuCl2·2H2O (1 mmol) and H2BTC (1 mmol) were firstly dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water and 10 mL of ethanol under stirring condition. After that, these two solutions were mixed and transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL) for the crystallization at 120 °C for 24 h. The orange solid was collected by centrifugation and washed several times with DMF and ethanol to remove the surfactant and remained reactants. This sample was named as HK. In contrast. o-CDs-M100 and o-CDs-HK were also prepared adding 10 % (molar rate) of CA in each MOFs precursor. The obtained samples were denoted as o-CDs-M100 and o-CDs-HK.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response experiment for crystallization process tracing of o-CDs-M101
ce:section.ce:para: All the test sample solutions were configured according to the volume and concentration of the solution at the time of synthesis, the pre-reaction samples were configured directly at room temperature. The samples at all temperatures during the reaction were obtained by terminating the reaction after the reaction had reached the temperature, and the post-reaction samples were the supernatants of the reaction products extracted directly after the reaction had ended. It was discovered that the emission peaks were related to the coordination state of Cr3+ after excitation at 205 nm [32].
Detailed information regarding the characterizations and measurements of materials above can be found in the Supporting Information.

The crystallization process scheme of CDs encapsulated MIL-101(Cr) (o-CDs-M101) via site-directly carbonization approach is illustrated in Fig. 1
a based on fluorescence response experiment. Firstly, several citric acid (CA) molecules with the carbon chain structure and plenty of –COOH/–OH groups are rapidly self-assembled into CA oligomers linked with hydrogen bonds and formed the CDs’ precursor. Then, these marginal –COOH groups of CA clusters were pre-coordinated with Cr3+ into Cr-CA (Fig. 1 a, step I). During this process, no typical fluorescence (FL) emission peaks are detected by FL spectrophotometer at the excitation wavelength of 205 nm [33] (Fig. 1 b). Secondly, MIL-101(Cr) nuclei are gradually formed based on these Cr-CA (Fig. 1 a, step II). The formed Cr-O clusters triggered fluorescence emission peaks at 350 and 475 nm (Fig. 1 b), whose intensity is clearly smaller compared to that of the solution pre-added BDC and Cr3+. This is because of the partial coordination of CA with Cr3+ and reduced the concentration of BDC-Cr3+ in solution. Then, a heating process (220 °C) was carried out to crystallize M101 and carbonized CA oligomers into CDs, simultaneously (Fig. 1 a, step III). Moreover, continuous monitoring chemical reaction via FL spectrophotometer (Fig. 1 c) was carried out for the nucleation process of MIL-101(Cr) crystal and CDs. The peaks of MOF ligand at 350 and 475 nm gradually disappear in heating stage before 110 °C, indicating the formation of MOF skeleton [34]. A new fluorescence peak began to appear at 462 nm (Fig. 1 c) representing the formation of CDs [35]. Notably, this peak gradually shifted towards blue position and increase intensity with heating time, indicating the increasing carbonization degree during the heating reaction [34,36].

To further investigate the ship-from-a-bottle effect of MOF pores, FL response test (Fig. 1 d) was carried out on samples of pure CDs from CA direct carbonization (CDs-CA), MIL-101(Cr), CD-Cr from CA coordinated Cr3+, and o-CDs-M101. As seen, pure MIL-101(Cr) shows very weak peaks intensity at 330 and 470 nm, while pure CDs-CA shows almost no FL response during this excitation wavelength due to their FL quenching from their polymerization into large particles without space confinement under the absence of polymerization inhibitor [36,37]. Differently, Cr-CDs sample exhibited much higher peak intensity at 375 and 462 nm, showing the typical CDs FL peaks [35]. It was speculated that the Cr3+ coordination with edged –COOH in CA may block CA polymerization into large particle to great extent. Thus, Cr-CDs still maintained CDs FL property without space confinement or adding no polymerization inhibitor. Notably, the encapsulated Cr-CDs in o-CDs-M101 shows the disappeared FL peaks at 375 nm, maybe attributing to nano-sized combination of CDs in porous MIL-101(Cr) and thus quenched fluorescence of CDs [36].

Fig. 2 shows crystalline structures of MOF and series MOF@CDs composites and the confined CDs distribution in MOFs. Original MIL-101(Cr) (Fig. 2 a) shows octahedron crystals with a uniform particle size of average 479 nm as previous report [17]. While o-CDs-M101 shows much smaller particle size of about 128 nm (Fig. 2 b). To insight CA effect on MOF crystal, different amounts of CA were added into MOF precursor solution and obtained three different o-CDs-M101 composites. Their SEM images ( Fig. S1
a-d) indicate that MOF’s particle size gradually decreased and grew into irregular morphology with increasing amount of CA varied from 5-20 % (molar percentage).

Furthermore, TEM image of o-CDs-M101 shows that amounts of dark dots were surrounded in MOF crystals with size distribution in the range of 2–3 nm from its high magnification TEM image (Fig. 2 c), which was comparable to the cage size of MIL-101(Cr). High resolution TEM image in Fig. 2 d clearly displayed their (100) carbon lattice fringe of CDs with a spacing of 2.1 Å [38,39]. This fully proved that these CA clusters formed CDs and confined inside of MIL-101(Cr) defects in o-CDs-M101 via one-pot synthesis. By comparison, post CDs synthesized p-CDs-M101 (Fig. 2 e) exhibits similar morphology to original MIL-101(Cr), demonstrating no influence of CDs post-growth on MOF crystal morphology. While CA modified CA-M101(Cr) in Fig. 2 f shows irregular morphology with smaller particle as contrasted with MIL-101(Cr). This may be attributed to the participation of CA coordination with Cr3+ in CA-M101(Cr) [40].

Fig. 2 g shows the crystallinity and crystal lattice orientation of original MIL-101(Cr) and their hybrid material. As can be seen from the XRD plots, pristine MIL-101(Cr) exhibits typical diffraction peaks at 5.1 and 9.0°, corresponding to (402) and (753) crystal planes of MIL-101(Cr) [29]. For the one-pot synthesized o-CDs-M101, it exhibited similar diffraction peak profile with stronger intensity to MIL-101(Cr). This manifested that the addition of CA molecule may facilitate the crystalline growth towards MIL-101(Cr) crystals [41]. On the contrary, p-CDs-M101 shows very weak intensity at main diffraction peak of (753) facet [40], indicating partially destroyed crystal structure in MIL-101(Cr) [19]. In our post-synthesis process, urea was used to control particle size of CDs precursor CA-clusters to make sure their entrance into MOF pores in dual-solvent system (detail shows in supporting information). However, basic property of urea etched and destroyed MOF crystallinity [14], which would bring out the sacrifice of MOF specific surface area. Similarly, CA modified CA-M101, as expected, displays clear decrease diffraction peaks intensity compared to o-CDs-M101 along with more amounts of amorphous MOFs. It may be ascribed to the large amount pre-coordination of CA with Cr3+, which was more prone to generating defective structure in MOF. Moreover, this approach also inhibited the formation of CA-cluster, and not avail to forming CDs in CA-M101. From the above mentioned, site-directly carbonization synthetic method could achieve defect pores to confine CDs growth within highly crystalline MIL-101(Cr).

To insight into chemical groups, FT-IR (Fig. 2 h) and Raman spectra (Fig. 2 i) were carried out and compared among original MIL-101(Cr) and their composites. As the diagram shows, FT-IR spectra show that all these samples exhibited the characteristic bands of MIL-101(Cr) at 3154 and 1559 cm−1 (C-H and C=C vibrations from benzene ring) and 1620 and 1402 cm−1 (asymmetric and symmetric telescoping vibrations of COO– from BDC), respectively [29]. In addition, the appeared wide peak at 3419 cm−1 mainly belonged to –OH vibration band from MOF’s adsorbed H2O [17]. By comparison, CA-M101 exhibited C-C, –CH2- vibration band from CA at 3000 cm−1 and the non-hydrogen bonding carboxylic acid O-H stretch at 2480 cm−1, referring that large amount of CA was implanted into MIL-101(Cr) [42,43]. In contrast, its peak intensity of 1559 cm−1 assigning to C=C from aromatic ring was decreased, which indicated a portion substitution of CA for BDC in CA-M101 skeleton [40].

While for o-CDs-M101 and p-CDs-M101, new peaks at 1302 and 1055 cm−1 appeared in their FT-IR spectra assigning to the existence of anhydride (C-O-C) group in CDs implanted MOFs. This group was indicated the formation of CDs in these two MOF composites [44]. Notably, o-CDs-M101 exhibits clearly stronger characterization peak of chain-structured anhydride at 1302 cm−1 [45], while p-CDs-M101 exhibited more cyclic-structured anhydride at 1055 cm−1. This may be attributed to their different growth conditions of CDs in MOFs. For o-CDs-M101, CA clusters were pre-formed before the MOFs crystal, which was trend to form chain clusters and then converted into chain-structured anhydride in CDs [15]. While for p-CDs-M101, CA and urea were introduced into MOF pores and preferentially reacted into cyclic-structured anhydride in CDs under confined conditions [46,47].

To identify the difference of carbon skeleton between o-CDs-M101 and other materials, Raman spectroscopy test was carried out and their results are shown in Fig. 2 i. For all materials, two typical peaks at 1420 and 1610 cm−1 represent C=C stretching of aromatic ring and O-C-O symmetric stretching in carboxylate in-phase [45], respectively. Differently, CA-M101 exhibited much weaker peak at these two positions, indicating that CA-M101 contained lower amount of benzene rings [48] due to CA participating coordination instead of BDC with Cr3+ in MIL-101(Cr). In addition, p-CDs-M101 shows very similar Raman spectrum to original MIL-101(Cr). By comparison, o-CDs-M101 possessed a new band at 540 cm−1, assigning to the existence of the ionic bond Cr3+O2– [49], demonstrating the connection between MOFs and CDs through Cr-COOH coordination in micro-environment.

Thermal stability of MOF is influenced by CA modification and CD implantation. Herein, TGA and DTG were applied on MIL-101(Cr) and its composites to insight these influences, and the resulting curves are illustrated in Fig. 2 j. It can be seen that the DTG curve of the original MIL-101(Cr) has four peaks at 375, 560, 650, 632 and 720 K, respectively [19]. These peaks represented H2O desorption, chemical bonding H2O, decarboxylation of free –COOH and structure decomposition [50]. By contrast, o-CDs-M101 displayed similar DTG profile except for right shift of peak representing decarboxylation of free –COOH from 632 to 643 K. This might be attributed to the binding of the CDs surface to the free –COOH of the MOF ligand, which inhibits the decarboxylation reaction. Apparently, it could be observed sharp weight loss peak at around 443 K in CA-M101, but no this peak appearing in o-CDs-M101. This indirectly proved CA conversion into CDs in o-CDs-M101 [50]. In addition, o-CDs-M101 displayed clearly lower amount of weight loss (67 %) than MIL-101(Cr) (75 %) and p-CDs-M101 (72 %), confirming the existence of chemically stable substances in MOF. These analyses indicated that CA clusters were successfully carbonized into CDs and formed special self-packaged structure in MIL-101(Cr) pores, manifesting better thermal adaptability and recombination compatibility [51]. N2 adsorption isotherms, micropore size distribution (analyzed by Horvath-Kawazoe method) and their textural parameters for MIL-101(Cr) and its hybrids are shown in Fig. 2 k and Table S2 . As seen in Fig. 2 k, MIL-101(Cr) exhibited the type I N2 sorption with multilevel adsorption behaviors due to its hierarchical porous structure. Its S BET was high up to 2895 m2/g ( Table S1 ) and micropore size distribution peak was concentrated at 7.5 and 10 Å [52,53]. On the contrary, o-CDs-M101 shows similar isotherm profile with slight increase of N2 adsorption uptake (S BET = 2770 m2/g) at very low pressure (P/P 0 < 0.003), and then decrease at higher pressures with a large hysteresis loop at the end of the isotherm compared to MIL-101(Cr). Obviously, its micropores were concentrated towards smaller pore size direction at peaks of 6.5 and 8.0 Å, which was not found in p-CDs-M101 and CA-M101. Combined with the above characterizations, these smaller micropores in o-CDs-M101 were mainly ascribed to the interfacial pores between these pore-trapped CDs and MOF during their one-pot synthesis. Moreover, CDs inevitably occupied partial mesoporous or even larger porous volume, and resulted in the decrease of BET surface area. Specifically, these interfacial ultra-micropores would be more beneficial to toluene capture via electrostatic interaction from mutual contribution between CDs and MOF pores [48]. By comparison, N2 adsorption quantity of p-CDs-M101 (S BET = 2553 m2/g) decreased more than o-CDs-M101 without appearance of new pore compared to MIL-101(Cr). Thus, these grown CDs could not efficiently promote toluene adsorption under low pressure ranges [54]. Similarly, compared with MIL-101(Cr), CA-M101 has a significantly lower SBET (2043 m2/g) but a similar microporous distribution, which suggests that the competitive coordination of CAs in its backbone disrupts the MOF crystals.

Fig. S2 and Table S1 show the XPS spectra and elemental compositions of MIL-101(Cr) and its composites to study the connection state of CD with MIL-101(Cr). As shown in Table S1
, it is worth mentioning that the element ratio of C/O and C/Cr in o-CDs-M101 and p-CDs-M101 clearly increased compared to MIL-101(Cr), indicating the existence of CDs in M101 due to higher content of C of CDs. Notably, o-CDs-M101 showed the highest elemental ratio of O/Cr in these MOFs belonging to the bridged Cr-O bonds between grown CDs and MOFs [48,55]. The high-resolution C 1 s spectra of these series MIL-101(Cr) composites are show in Fig. S2
b. By comparison, original MIL-101(Cr) exhibited peak area ratio of C-C (C sp2 and C sp3)/C-O of 11.6 (Fig. 2 l), while this peak area ratio (7.5) of CA-M101 was less than those of the original MIL-101(Cr). This was mainly because of more explosion of C-O bonds in CA-M101 from CA implanting [40]. Furthermore, o-CDs-M101 exhibits higher C-C/C-O ratio than CA-M101, which was attributed to the carbonization of CDs in o-CDs-M101. Ratio of p-CDs-M101 was the highest, which indicated higher carbonatation degree p-CDs of due to the dry-state calcination. Based on the result of FTIR spectra, more chain structure anhydride was detected in o-CDs-M101 and more cyclic anhydride was in p-CDs-M101, indicating that the one-pot synthesis method has lower carbonatation degree and higher surface functional groups for connections between CDs and MOF. Therefore, the formation of connections would facilitate electron transfer pathways from o-CDs to MOF [56].

Cr 2p spectrum in Fig. S2
c and O 1p spectrums in Fig. S2
d showed that Cr-O peak, The o-CDs-M101 showed the binding energy (BE) position of Cr-O 0.1 eV shifting towards lower BE compared to MIL-101(Cr), which was caused by the extra electron donation from the confined grown CDs through Cr-O bonds[57,58]. Comparing to Cr-O clusters of original MIL-101(Cr), addition of CA generated defective Cr-O clusters, which cause the rise of electron density of Cr (III) and corresponding decrease electron density of O of Cr-O in o-CDs-M101[59]. Thus, site-directly carbonized CDs has coordination bonds of Cr-O-C in o-CDs-M101 and generated an electron-rich ultra micropore interface by CDs, which is a appropriate position for energy exchange of photothermal ad/desorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of toluene enhanced by CDs “one-pot site nucleation”
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 3 illustrates the adsorption capacities of toluene vapor on pristine M101, p-CDs-M101, CA-M101, and o-CDs-M101 at 298 K. The adsorption isotherms for all four materials exhibited a type-I profile, characterized by two-stage platforms and reaching adsorption saturation at P/P 0 = 0.5 ( Fig. S3
). The maximum adsorbed uptake for toluene (Q max, P/P 0 = 0.5) followed the order: MIL-101(Cr) (13.5 mmol/g) > p-CDs-M101 (11.0 mmol/g) > o-CDs-M101 (9.9 mmol/g) > CA-M101 (7.6 mmol/g). Compared to MIL-101(Cr), the other materials demonstrated relatively lower toluene uptakes due to their reduced pore volume resulting from different modification methods. Among these, o-CDs-M101 prepared via one-pot CDs growth exhibited even lower toluene uptake compared to p-CDs-M101, primarily due to higher volume occupation by CDs. This observation is consistent with our TG (Fig. 2 j), N2 adsorption (Fig. 2 k) and XPS (Fig. 2 l) characterizations. However, the two-stage profile of o-CDs-M101 shifted towards lower P/P 0 values, indicating its higher affinity for toluene compared to p-CDs-M101 and MIL-101(Cr) [21].

To further investigate the toluene capture performance, the toluene adsorption isotherms at ultra-low pressure in the range of 0.001–0.1 (toluene concentration lower than 2000 mg/m3) were amplified and depicted in Fig. 3 a. As depicted, MIL-101(Cr) exhibited a low adsorption of 1.1 mmol/g at P/P 0 = 0.01 (corresponding to a reference value for 400 ppm toluene [60]). Conversely, o-CDs-MIL-101(Cr) reached an adsorption quantity of up to 4.4 mmol/g at P/P 0 = 0.01, which surpasses the uptake of MIL-101(Cr) in the low-pressure region. Additionally, a new third stage appeared in the range of P/P 0 = 0.004–0.015 for o-CDs-MIL-101(Cr). According to the analysis of the N2 adsorption isotherm, adjustment of pore size by implanting CDs synchronously created composite defective interfaces between MIL-101(Cr) and CDs. In addition, non-polar sites on CDs may provide additional and strong adsorption sites towards toluene having high uptakes even under low concentrations.

In comparison, p-CDs-M101 demonstrated a slight increase in adsorption capacity (1.5 mmol/g), suggesting that the post-synthesis method had a minor impact on the low-pressure toluene adsorption of MIL-101(Cr), which is attributed to the difficulty of p-CDs growing into ultra-micropores. For CA-M101, the toluene low-pressure adsorption capacity was only 2.9 mmol/g, significantly lower than that of o-CDs-MIL-101(Cr). Unlike o-CDs-M101, CA-M101 was fabricated without the formation of CDs in MOF and may easily form coordination defects resulting in the decrease of MOF’s BET surface area with lower toluene adsorption uptakes.

In Fig. 3 b, the adsorption capacities at 298 K of water vapor on pristine MIL-101(Cr), p-CDs-M101, CA-M101, and o-CDs-M101 are depicted. The water adsorption isotherms exhibited a characteristic “S-shaped” profile containing three distinct stages of water adsorption. In the first stage (0–35 % RH), the MOFs displayed hydrophobic properties with low absorption of water vapor due to limited unsaturated metal sites. Subsequently, at the second stage (35–50 % RH), there was a rapid increase in water adsorption as the micropores of the MOFs became filled. Finally, at the third stage, water is saturated with adsorption capacity. This observation suggests that water adsorption attains its saturation inflection point. Similar to the adsorption isotherms for toluene, all four materials exhibited a type-I profile for water adsorption, characterized by two-stage platforms and reaching adsorption saturation at P/P 0 = 0.99. The maximum adsorbed uptake for water followed the order: MIL-101(Cr) (1.41 mmol/g) > p-CDs-M101 (1.43 mmol/g) > CA-M101 (1.15 mmol/g) > o-CDs-M101 (0.93 mmol/g). Based on the reference value of the original material ( Table S3 ), the adsorption capacity and BET surface area data were normalized. It is observed that o-CDs significantly reduces the water absorption affinity of MIL-101(Cr). This indicates that the hydrophobic effect within the MOF pores is greatly improved by incorporating o-CDs [14,19,61].

The surface properties of adsorbents were investigated through a TPD experiment to determine their relative adsorption affinity for toluene. Fig. 3 c shows that their TPD curves exhibited a single desorption peak for both toluene and water at a heating rate of 5 K/min. The desorption peak temperatures (T dp) for toluene followed the order in Fig. 3 c-left: MIL-101(Cr) (107 °C) < o-CDs-M101 (110 °C) < p-CDs-M101 (117 °C) < CA-M101 (124 °C). Among which, o-CDs-M101 showed lower T dp than p-CDs-M101 and CA-M101 for toluene desorption. Usually, T dp in TPD is mainly represented for the adsorption affinity between adsorbate (toluene) and adsorbent (MOFs), and the impact of heat transfer on thermal desorption rate is often overlooked entirely. However, in our case, CDs introduction can efficiently promote thermal transfer within o-CDs-M101. This accelerated toluene molecules to gain heat and then desorb from o-CDs-M101 pores, and resulted in the decrease T dp (desorption activation energy) in their TPD curves. That explains the conflict between ultra-high toluene uptake under low pressure and relative lower toluene desorption activation in o-CDs-M101.

For H2O-TPD profiles on the right side of Fig. 3 c, MIL-101(Cr) exhibited the largest desorption peak area and a temperature of 52 °C for toluene. In the case of CA-M101 and p-CDs-M101, the peak temperature for H2O-TPD was 65 and 48 °C, respectively. This suggested that CA modification made MIL-101(Cr) more hydrophilic while p-CDs makes it more hydrophobic. By comparison, o-CDs-M101 had the lowest desorption peak temperature for H2O, indicating its superior hydrophobic properties [62]. Water contact angle (WCA) test in Fig. S4 confirmed that o-CDs-M101 (WCA = 63.1°) had much higher hydrophobicity than MIL-101(Cr) (WCA = 23.3°), confirming the successful introduction of o-CDs as a hydrophobic shield in MIL-101(Cr) [63].

Furthermore, Fig. 3 d depicts the adsorption kinetics curves and adsorption rates of toluene adsorption over MIL-101(Cr) and o-CDs-M101. These samples reached adsorption equilibrium in 45 min (MIL-101(Cr) and 25 min (o-CDs-M101). To determine the adsorption rate of toluene on these four samples, the general diffusion model (Eq. (S1)) was utilized to fit the toluene adsorption kinetics curve and adsorption rate was calculated. The calculated adsorption rate (k a) for MIL-101(Cr) was determined to be 0.15⋅min−1, while o-CDs-M101 exhibited the k a value of 1.3 times of MIL-101(Cr). As mentioned above, edge groups of CDs in o-CDs-M101 were preferentially coordinated with Cr3+ to form mesoporous defects on the interface between CDs and microporous MIL-101. This fabricated hierarchical pore structure facilitated the VOCs diffusion from bulk to MOF cages [64]. Consequently, o-CDs-M101 exhibits stronger adsorption interactions with toluene and facilitates faster diffusion into the internal micropore environment [65], thereby enhancing toluene adsorption efficiency.

To gain insights into the adsorption dynamic behavior and adsorption performance of toluene under humidity, breakthrough curves were generated by passing toluene through a fixed bed containing MIL-101(Cr) samples and their composites at 298 K and relative humidity (RH) of 10 % and 60 % (Fig. 3 e). The working and equilibrium adsorbed amounts of toluene on these adsorbents were estimated using Eq. (S4) and are presented in Table S3 .

With increasing RH, it was observed that the breakthrough time of toluene vapor decreased on MIL-101(Cr), indicating adsorption capacity dropping due to the hindered of some adsorption sites by water molecules. However, o-CDs-M101 exhibited a reduced decrease in toluene capacity of work (323 mg/g) at 10 % RH. Notably, even at a higher RH of 60 %, o-CDs-M101 maintained a working capacity of toluene adsorption of 278 mg/g, which corresponds to approximately 2.6 times that of pure MIL-101(Cr). It is attributed to the self-packaged carbon dots within o-CDs-M101, which effectively hindered the entrance of water vapors [50]. The breakthrough time (t b) of o-CDs-M101 (74 min) was about 1.6 times longer than that of original MIL-101(Cr) (46 min) at 10 % RH and the t b of o-CDs-M101 (80 min) was about 2.8 times longer than that of the original MIL-101(Cr) at 60 % RH. This implies that hydrophobic groups of carbon dots significantly enhanced the toluene/water-competitive adsorption on MIL-101(Cr). Moreover, the ratio of working capacity to equilibrium capacity (Q w/Q e) was calculated and shown in Table S3 . The total quantities of the adsorption sites in the adsorbent and the sites in the adsorbent with a strong affinity for toluene are corresponding to Q w and Q e respectively. As RH increased, both the Q e and Q w decreased two adsorbents, resulting in a gradual increase in the utilization ratio (Q w/Q e) of the two adsorbents. However, the Q w/Q e of o-CDs-M101 increased faster with rising RH compared to that of MIL-101(Cr), which may since o-CDs-M101 possessed a higher hydrophobic surface than parent MIL-101(Cr).

Furthermore, the Yoon and Nelson (Y-N) model, described by Eq. (S5), was employed to characterize the dynamic adsorption behavior towards toluene on both the one-pot synthesized o-CDs-M101 nanohybrid and the synthesized MIL-101.The fitting profiles and parameters are presented in Fig. S5
a and Table S4 . At both RH levels, the τ value for o-CDs-M101 was notably higher MIL-101(Cr) τ value indicating its superior working capacity. Simultaneously, the rate constant kb for toluene adsorption on the four adsorbents gradually increased with increasing RH. Comparison reveals that the kb of o-CDs-M101 exhibited a faster increase with rising RH compared to that of MIL-101(Cr). This can be attributed to preference of water molecules to the channels of the parent MIL-101(Cr) as RH increased and led to pore blockage. Excessive water molecules within the pores formed larger clusters, impeding the mass transfer of toluene. For o-CDs-M101, the introduction of CDs as a hydrophobic shell effectively hindered the formation of larger water clusters in the pore channels and narrowed pore size, which then enhanced its affinity for toluene adsorption compared to MIL-101(Cr). The resulting hydrophobicity and more affinity to toluene enhanced the adsorption rate of o-CDs-M101 toward toluene [66]. In addition, the one-pot method composite has better basic properties and adsorption performance than the dual-solvent method composite, demonstrates the subtleties of “site-directly carbonization ” approach.

As shown in Fig. S5
b, XRD pattern before and after adsorption of p-CDs-M101 and o-CDs-M101 bring into correspondence. Static and dynamic adsorption uptakes toward toluene for 5 cycles Fig. S5
c-d drops only 1 % and 4 %, manifesting high structure and performance stabilities of o-CDs-M101 [67].

To evaluate the adsorption efficiency of o-CDs-M101, comparison of toluene adsorption isotherm at low pressure range on many reported state-of-the-art porous adsorbents such as other MOFs (NH2-MIL-125, HKUST-1) and other modified MIL-101(Cr) at 298 K temperature and low pressures (P < 50 Pa) [21,64] was shown in Fig. 3 f. For o-CDs-M101, it displayed much higher adsorption capacity of toluene (3.0–4.5 mmol/g) compared to some state-of-the-art porous adsorbents under the pressure range of 20–40 Pa. This capacity was about 3.2–4.1 times that of MIL-101(Cr) and 1.4–3.6 times that of NH2-MIL-125, HKUST-1, MIL-101. Notably, o-CDs-M101 showed better adsorption capacity for toluene than currently reported MOF adsorbents in comprehensive aspects (Fig. 3 g and Table S5 ). Consequently, hydrophobic CDs were encapsulated into MOF cages through O-Cr bonding and formed abundant interface and new hierarchical pores. Inner cores of CDs supplied more non-polar adsorption sites, and thus represented preferential adsorption with high uptake towards toluene via π-π interaction compared towards H2O. It is evident that o-CDs-M101 not only prevented water adsorption but also facilitated toluene diffusion within the pores [64] when compared to other samples. Moreover, the hydrophobic aromatic rings support the water resistance by forming interface with MOFs defective sites. As a result, water diffusion decreased while toluene diffusion increased in o-CDs-M101.

To clarify the competitive adsorption mechanism of the composite, theoretical simulation via Density Functional Theory (DFT) was performed for bond lengths and system exchange in energy after toluene adsorption. Preferential adsorption sites of toluene on MIL-101, CA-M101 and o-CDs-M101 are presented in Fig. 3 h-j. The adsorption characteristics of toluene molecules were test to determine their stable configuration and associated energy when bound to the metallic cores of various adsorbents.

For each scenario, various arrangements were evaluated for the initial or modified metallic sites at different locations. The outcomes were analyzed using quantum mechanics principles outlined in Eq. (S6). Fig. 3 h-j illustrates the most stable arrangement for C7H8 adsorption energy (E ad) on each adsorbent's active site. The complex formed between a toluene molecule and the Cr site of MIL-101(Cr) exhibits an E ad of −18.2 kJ/mol with an interaction distance of 3.342 Å. When toluene binds to the hydrogen site of CA-M101, the E ad increases to −19.7 kJ/mol, and the interaction distance shortens to 2.887 Å. These findings suggest that incorporating CA enhances the adsorptive strength of MIL-101(Cr) towards toluene molecules [19,61]. In Fig. 3 j, the complex where a toluene molecule attaches to the π site of o-CDs-M101 shows a higher E ad (−44.5 kJ/mol) and a shorter interaction distance (3.487 Å), indicating that introducing basic π sites into MIL-101 through CDs improves its adsorption capacity for toluene vapor [17]. Overall, density functional theory simulations confirm that site-directly carbonized CDs heightens o-CDs-M101′s attraction to toluene during the adsorption process.

Unlike conventional thermal desorption, phot-assisted strategy was proposed to be use for toluene desorption from our designed o-CDs-M101 considering its thermal and non-thermal effects. Thermal and photo synergistic effects on desorption performances of o-CDs-M101, including desorption efficiency, desorption kinetics and toluene concentration ratio are shown in (Fig. 4
a-e). Fig. 4 a exhibited dynamic desorption of toluene on o-CDs-M101 under different light power of 370, 460 and 600 mW [68]. As seen in inset of Fig. 4 a, the temperature rise curves of o-CDs-M101 with different light irradiation powers are given. It was found that all the samples’ temperatures were sharply increased and reached a constant temperature, which was almost proportional to the corresponding light irradiation power. As expected, desorption curves showed an increasing desorption ratio (q d/q a) from 77.3 to 96.4 % but similar desorption kinetics of toluene with light power from 370 to 460 mW. The result calculations were listed in supporting information (SI section 1.2-3). However, its desorption ratio was not continuously increased when light power reached 600 mW. An increase in light power enables CDs to absorb more light energy, generating more heat and excited electrons, thus accelerating the desorption of toluene molecules from the adsorption sites. However, when the light power is excessively high (e.g., 600 mW), issues such as local overheating-induced structural changes of the adsorbent or saturation of energy utilization efficiency may occur, resulting in the desorption ratio ceasing to increase continuously. Considering the minimum energy consumption, light irradiation power of 460 mW was chosen in the next photo-thermal desorption.

Furthermore, photo and thermal synergistic effects on toluene desorption performance of o-CDs-M101 was demonstrated in Fig. 4 b-c by monitoring its photo-thermal and pure thermal desorption under different controlled desorption temperatures (T d = 30, 80 and 110 °C). Under light trigger condition in Fig. 4 b, the desorption ratio of q d/q a was almost zero at 30 °C, indicating no occurrence of toluene desorption from o-CDs-M101. Increase of the removal ratio to 31.7 % within 60 min achieved at 80 °C. Furthermore, its toluene desorption ratio reached up to 96.4 % under light irradiation to 110 °C within 60 min. By comparison, desorption kinetic behavior in Fig. 4 c showed a great discrepancy at similar temperatures under pure heating condition. It can be observed that almost no desorbed toluene molecules were detected from 30 to 80 °C. As the temperature increased to 110 °C, only 49.5 % toluene was desorbed from o-CDs-M101 even the desorption time reaching 60 min. The photothermal synergistic efficiency (k s) is calculated by following equation:

q PT: Photothermal desorption quantity;
q P: Photothermal desorption quantity after heat removal;
q T: Desorption quantities by pure thermal-desorption.

k s was 1.9 under light-driven desorption, indicating that light-irradiation presented faster energy transfer efficiency to strip toluene from o-CDs-M101 with decreased desorption activation energy [69]. Next, their desorption rate (k d) and toluene concentration ratio (R c) at 110 °C was calculated and shown in Fig. 4 d-e. As seen, k d of photothermal (PT) desorption was up to 3.4 times of thermal (T) desorption (Fig. 4 d), and R c of PT desorption was 3.8 times higher than that of T desorption (Fig. 4 e). t90 is the time when the desorption amount is 90 % of the total desorption amount.

As the adsorption performance mentioned above, o-CDs-M101 also did well in the desorption after adsorption under humidity. The dynamic photothermal desorption efficiency of o-CDs-M101(removal ratio is 96.4 %) was still high and stable. Desorption rate at RH = 60 % raised to 2.5 times of RH = 5 % (Fig. 4 f). H2O promoting in the release of toluene molecules, temperature has risen 12 °C and energy cost reduced 60 % of RH = 5 % (Fig. 4 g). But o-CDs-M101′s hydrophobic barrier prevents water from forming molecule clusters. The results indicated that the desorption rate of toluene adsorbed in o-CDs-M101 was significantly improve under higher RH (60 %), and the photo-thermal energy transfer level mainly control the removal rate of toluene adsorbed in o-CDs-M101. Overall, these findings suggest that confined-grown CDs significantly reduced desorption energy barrier and thus enhanced desorption efficiency of MIL-101(Cr), resulting fast desorption rate and high enrichment concentration of toluene.

To confirm the feasibility of the synthetic method for different MOFs, HKUST-1 and MIL-100(Fe), as two typical MOFs, were used to obtain o-CDs-HK and o-CDs-M100 after the same one-pot synthesis. XRD analysis provided evidence for the successful formation of MOFs crystals with high crystallinity ( Fig. S6 ). The characteristic peak of HKUST-1 and MIL-100(Fe) was shift to left in the XRD patterns affected by CDs, indicating the CDs coverage of MOFs skeleton. Desorption/adsorption efficiency (Fig. 4 h) shows that three chosen MOFs (HKUST-1, MIL-100(Fe) and MIL-101(Cr)) all have enhanced desorption efficiency (desorption ratio of 1.24, 1.19 and 1.13 times of original MOFs respectively) due to encapsulation of carbon quantum dots under photothermal conditions. Fig. 4 i-k shows photothermal desorption curves of toluene on HKUST-1, o-CDs-HK, MIL-100(Fe), o-CDs-M100, MIL-101(Cr) and o-CDs-M101(Cr). Despite differences in synthesis temperatures and conditions of these MOFs, opto-rich effect of carbon dots on the photothermal desorption also brings increase of 13 %-24 % desorption ratio compared to the original MOFs.

To insight CDs effects on photo-thermal desorption performance in o-CDs-M101, thermal and non-thermal effects were systematically explored via the detection of photothermal conversion, photogenerated electron and energy transfer. Firstly, thermal conductivity and thermal transfer coefficient (cooling and heating) were characterized and shown in Fig. S7-
9. By comparison, o-CDs-M101 was measured to have 1.42 and 1.14 times higher of thermal conductivities and thermal transfer efficiency than MIL-101(Cr), inferring that the introduction of CDs can improve pure heat transfer of MIL-101(Cr) to a certain extent. However, it does not play a major role in promoting the desorption efficiency.

Moreover, photo-generated thermal effect was monitored via the temperature of MOFs at different times rising under light irradiation and shown in Fig. 5
a. o-CDs-M101 showed much faster temperature rising rate (rT ) with significantly higher equilibrium temperature (about 14 °C of temperature discrepancy) under same light irradiation within only 10 min. Thus, CDs inside of MIL-101(Cr) was proved to efficiently capture light energy and convert it into heat, and presented advanced photo-thermal effects on toluene desorption [32]. Besides, non-thermal effects, including light absorption and photoelectric conversion performance, were also measured via UV–vis spectra and photocurrent response on these two adsorbents. As shown in Fig. 5 b and c, the introduction of CDs clearly brought a new peak at 700 nm and intensified light absorbance region from 250 to 750 nm, which had average increment of 35 %-fold photo-current density of MIL-101(Cr). After adsorption of toluene, the new peak at 700 nm disappeared from the spectra of o-CDs-M101, manifesting that toluene adsorption has triggered CDs structure changes [27,70]. The promoted ability of photoelectric conversion may be contributed from high photocatalytic activity and photoelectron separation efficiency with the aid of CDs in MIL-101(Cr) pores.

In this work, site-directly carbonized CDs in o-CDs-M101 exhibited merits of high dispersion and nano-scale size to boost light harvesting in pores of MIL-101(Cr) (SEM&TEM Fig. 2 a-f).Moreover, CDs coordinated with Cr sites (Raman Fig. 2 i) of MIL-101(Cr) and generated more amount of defective Cr-O clusters in MOFs (XPS Fig. S2
a-d), which efficiently boost photoelectric conversion from adsorbed light energy and the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs within MOF (Fig. 5 c-d). Thirdly, CDs was also observed to emit fluorescence in MOF pores, which was continuously converted into molecular vibration and thus facilitated the escaping of adsorbed toluene from o-CDs-M101 [28]. From the above analysis, it can be concluded that the meso-cages MIL-101(Cr) could provide abundant pores for energy radiation [71]. When light was introduced into o-CDs-M101, it was easier to disperse in MOF pore and then harvest by site-directly carbonized CDs. After that, the light energy triggered photo-thermal and photo-electric conversion by o-CDs-M101, and then accelerated transferred to adsorbed toluene around CDs in MOF pore cages for their desorption. Thus, o-CDs-M101 exhibited advanced energy utilization for highly efficient toluene desorption via photo synergistic effect of thermal and non-thermal mechanism, enrich intrinsic value of the sorbent.

In summary, CDs were encapsulated in MIL-101(Cr) through “site-directly carbonization” ship-from-a-bottle approach for enhancing synergistic accelerations of adsorption and photo-assisted desorption of toluene. During adsorption process, these encapsulated CDs can work as “VOCs affinity sites” and “hydrophobic shield” to attract toluene and resist H2O, which efficiently maintain high toluene adsorption uptake under humidity conditions. It achieved a 40 % increase of toluene uptake (3.6 mmol/g) of original MIL-101(Cr) under low concentration and high humidity (400 ppm, RH = 60 %). While, during the photo-assisted desorption process, CDs in MOFs switch to “photothermic spots” and rapidly generate heat and electrons to attack these adsorbed VOCs on them, achieving fast toluene desorption. The strong adsorption site of o-CDs is the “photothermal spot” and o-CDs, as “transfer station for absorption/desorption enhancement”, provides advantages for heat and mass transfer of MOF adsorbents in photothermal desorption. The desorption performance shows that the photothermal desorption ratio of o-CDs-M101 increased from 49.5 % (thermal desorption) to 94.6 % within 60 min, exhibiting a desorption rate (0.037 s−1) and concentration ratio (8.07) up to 3.4 and 3.8 times, respectively, compared to thermal desorption process. By calculation, our photothermal synergistic efficiency (k s) was about 1.9, which doubled the efficiency of thermal desorption with the same energy consumption. Interestingly, these adsorbed water molecules would further dramatically accelerate toluene desorption from MOFs, increasing by 2.5 times compared with the absence of water. The desorption efficiency of o-CDs@M101 remained after five cycles. Furthermore, this approach enabled the successful photo-assisted desorption accelerating of other two other typical MOFs, including HKUST-1 and MIL-100(Fe). Hence, this introduction of photothermal sites can be regarded as ideal approach for MOF regeneration with high efficiency and low energy consumption, especially under low concentration and highly humid environments.
